title: Slow-roast shoulder of lamb
subtitle: Six hours in the oven, falling off the bone
excerpt: Lamb shoulder cooked low and slow until the meat collapses and can be pulled apart with two forks. No carving required. The long cook renders the fat, softens the collagen, and produces a deeply savoury, yielding roast that is far more forgiving than a leg.
sourceNotes: Slow-roasted shoulder has become the standard alternative to the traditional leg in British home cooking. The shoulder contains more connective tissue and fat than the leg, making it ideal for long, low cooking. It cannot be overcooked in the way a leg can — the worst outcome is meat that is even more tender.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Season and stud the day before. This dish reheats exceptionally well — cook it a day ahead and reheat covered at 160°C for 40 minutes.

content.text: A bone-in shoulder of around 2 kg feeds six generously when pulled. The shoulder is not carved — after six hours at low heat the meat simply falls away from the bone in chunks. This makes it far more accessible than a leg roast, and the end result is richer.

Six hours at 160°C is the standard. At 4 hours the meat is cooked through but still holds its structure. By hour six it will collapse completely. Both are correct depending on whether you want sliceable or pullable. The shoulder will not turn dry if you go longer.

Heat the oven to 220°C. Using a small sharp knife, make 18 deep incisions all over the {{lamb-shoulder}}. Push a sliver of {{garlic}} and a small piece of {{rosemary}} into each incision. Rub all over with {{olive-oil}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Place in a deep roasting tin and roast at 220°C for 20 minutes to colour the outside. Cover tightly with two layers of foil. Reduce the oven to 160°C and roast for 5 to 6 hours, until the meat is completely tender and falling from the bone. Remove the foil for the last 20 minutes to crisp the surface. Transfer to a warm board, tent loosely with foil, and rest for 20 minutes before pulling apart with two forks.

items.symptom: Meat dry and stringy rather than yielding
items.cause: Foil seal broken during cooking, or oven too high
items.fix: The foil must form a tight seal to trap the steam. Even a small gap will dry the meat out. Check the seal before putting it back in

items.symptom: Surface pale and stewed-looking rather than browned
items.cause: Skipped the initial 220°C blast, or foil not removed for the final period
items.fix: Both the initial blast and the uncovered final 20 minutes are essential for colour. If the surface is still pale after the last 20 minutes, increase to 200°C for a further 10 minutes

items.cause: Slivers pushed into the surface rather than deep into the flesh
items.fix: Make the incisions at least 3 cm deep. The aromatics must sit inside the muscle to survive the 6-hour cook without burning

content.text: The slow-roast shoulder has displaced the leg in many British households because it requires almost no skill and delivers a more forgiving result. It suits a Sunday lunch where you need to put it in the oven at 9am and forget it until 3pm. The pulled-meat texture also works better in sandwiches and flatbreads with leftover lamb than carved slices from a leg.
